nativeRange.habitat: Small, shallow, slow-moving jungle tributaries and pools with sandy substrate, dense leaf litter, submerged wood, and overhanging vegetation; blackwater or near-blackwater conditions with very soft, acidic water

decorPreferences: caves (coconut shells, clay pots, small terracotta caves)
multiple sight-line breaks for female territories

diet.notes: Primarily micropredatory; feeds on benthic invertebrates in the wild. Accepts live and frozen foods readily — baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), daphnia, moina, bloodworms (chironomid larvae), and cyclops are ideal staples. Captive-raised specimens adapt to high-quality small pellets and gel foods. Feed small amounts multiple times daily; variety is key to long-term health. Some individuals actively hunt small snails. Avoid overfeeding tubifex and red mosquito larvae.

goodWith: small, upper-water tetras (e.g., ember tetras, neon tetras)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma males (without large, divided territory)
A. nijsseni (hybridization risk; offspring are deformed)
dwarf shrimp (will be eaten or harassed)
boisterous fast-swimming fish that disturb cave sites

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen thumbnail from FishBase photo gallery (species ID 62348). Full-res available at /photos/ThumbnailsSummary.php?ID=62348. Check license before use.
Female specimen thumbnail from FishBase photo gallery (species ID 62348). Shows typical yellow female coloration.

summary: One of the most visually spectacular dwarf cichlids — males display striking electric-blue flanks, vivid yellow ventrals, and bold black markings with a red-ringed tail. Described formally in 2004 from Peru's Río Huallaga system; member of the nijsseni group. Cave-spawner with haremic social structure; females are devoted mothers. Easier to keep than related A. nijsseni.
careNotes:
Apistogramma baenschi, the Inca Dwarf Cichlid, is considered among the more accessible species in the genus despite its exacting soft-water requirements. Males are significantly larger (up to 5.5 cm) than females (~3.5 cm) and display some of the most vivid coloration in the entire Apistogramma genus: an electric-blue or turquoise body, contrasting yellow undersides, bold dark vertical bars, and a distinctive red or red-orange ring on the caudal fin. Formally described by Römer et al. in 2004 — prior to this, the fish circulated in the trade as Apistogramma sp. 'Inka' or 'Inka 50' and was sometimes mislabelled as A. nijsseni.

Wild fish originate from small, shallow tributaries of the Río Huallaga system in northern Peru (Shanushi and Paranapura drainages, including the Río Huayabamba drainage), characterised by extremely soft, acidic blackwater conditions (pH 4–6.5, GH near zero). Aquarium-bred specimens show greater tolerance for moderately neutral water (up to pH ~7.0) but still thrive best below pH 6.5. Use RO water cut with a small amount of tap water, or condition with catappa leaves and peat. Carbonate hardness should be negligible.

The species is naturally haremic: in the wild one male occupies a territory encompassing the smaller territories of multiple females. In a well-scaped 80+ cm tank, a trio (one male, two females) works well. Each female requires a dedicated cave site out of sight of the other females — provide one cave per female plus a spare. A 20-gallon long (75 L) tank suits a pair; a harem benefits from 112 L or more. Males are not overly aggressive toward non-cichlid tankmates; small upper-water fish (tetras, pencilfish) serve as dither fish and help keep the cichlids from remaining hidden.

Confusion with A. nijsseni (Panda Apisto): both share the nijsseni species group and superficially similar coloration. A. nijsseni females display a large dark blotch covering much of the lower flank, while A. baenschi females show discrete vertical bars. A. nijsseni is also considerably more demanding of strict blackwater conditions and is regarded as harder to breed. The two species can hybridize if kept together, producing infertile or malformed offspring — keep them separate.

Water temperature 22–26°C is optimal; brief excursions to 28°C are tolerated. Filtration should be gentle (sponge filter preferred) with no strong currents near cave entrances. Perform 25–30% water changes weekly using pre-conditioned soft water to avoid pH swings. Avoid copper-based medications; while not formally listed as copper-sensitive, small soft-water cichlids are generally sensitive to heavy metals.
breedingNotes:
Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care and haremic social structure. Females select dark, enclosed spawning sites (coconut huts, clay caves, overturned flowerpots) and deposit eggs on the inner ceiling or walls. Clutch size is typically 30–80 eggs. Eggs hatch in 36–72 hours depending on temperature; fry become free-swimming 5–6 days later. The female guards and tends the fry aggressively, chasing off all intruders including the male. In smaller tanks the male may need to be removed after spawning to prevent female stress.

Soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5, GH <3) is important for egg development and fry survival; harder water may cause egg fungusing or fry deformities. First foods for newly free-swimming fry: freshly hatched Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, and paramecia. Fry are relatively large and easy to raise; advance to finely crushed dry foods after 1–2 weeks. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 5–6 months. A. baenschi females are noted to be excellent, attentive mothers and generally more reliable breeders than A. nijsseni. Breeding difficulty is considered easy to moderate for aquarium-raised stock.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma baenschi
primary.notes: Primary species profile. Provided water parameters (20–26°C, pH 4.0–6.5, hardness 18–90 ppm), adult size (males 5.5 cm, females 4 cm), minimum tank dimensions (80×30 cm), diet (live/frozen invertebrates), breeding details (egg hatch 36–72 h, fry free-swimming +5–6 days, maturity 5–6 months), native range (Huallaga system, Shanushi and Paranapura drainages), and nijsseni-group classification.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma baenschi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Geophaginae), native range (South America: Peru), max male length (5.3 cm SL), formal description year (Römer et al., 2004), and IUCN status (Least Concern, assessed 2014). Provided image candidate URLs.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma care; applied genus-level guidance on tank size (20 gal / 75 L minimum for pair), water parameters (temperature 27–28°C, somewhat acidic to neutral pH, GH 4–8 dGH), diet, cave requirements, and compatible tankmates. A. baenschi-specific tank temperatures are slightly cooler per SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: DwarfCichlid.com — Apistogramma baenschi
additional.notes: Species-specific hobbyist reference. Provided natural pH range (4.1–6.3), note on adaptability of captive-bred stock, substrate preference (fine sand, sand-sifting), snail-eating behavior, female brood care quality ('excellent mothers'), fry schedule (hatch 48–72 h, free-swimming +4–5 days), male size (3 inches / ~7.5 cm including fins), and comparison with A. nijsseni (more demanding, harder to breed; hybridization produces 'deformed and ugly offspring').

additional.name: AquaInfo — Apistogramma baenschi
additional.notes: Dutch aquarium resource. Confirmed water parameters (20–26°C, pH 4–6.5, GH 0–4), native range (Río Huallaga, Río Paranapura, Río Shanushi, northwestern Peru), harem social structure, male coloration (bluish base, yellow-to-red tail ring), female coloration (yellow, turning bright yellow with black stripes when breeding), and breeding ease ('relatively easy').

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner with strong maternal brood care; eggs hatch 36–72 h, fry free-swimming in 5–6 days. Requires soft acidic water (pH 5.0–6.5, GH <3) for successful egg development. Aquarium-bred stock is reliable; wild-caught stock demands stricter blackwater conditions.
